topic: Context 3: School Life, Studies and the World of Work
subject: french

items.q: How do you say 'I study maths' in French?
items.why: Students drop the article like English. School subjects in French take the DEFINITE article: 'j'aime le français', 'j'étudie les sciences', 'je déteste l'histoire'. This is the rule for general nouns.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to visit Paris' (near future) in French?
items.why: Students add 'à' between aller and the infinitive. Futur proche structure is ALLER (conjugated) + INFINITIVE, with no preposition: 'je vais manger', 'je vais aller', 'je vais partir'.

items.q: How do you describe weather in the past, e.g. 'it was sunny'?
items.misconception: Passé composé - 'Il a fait beau' (which suggests a completed event).
items.why: Weather descriptions in past narratives use IMPERFECT (ongoing state/description). Passé composé is for completed events. 'Quand je suis arrivé, il faisait beau' = ongoing weather backdrop; arrived = completed action.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Les maths sont plus difficiles que l'anglais.
items.misconception: Les maths est plus difficile que l'anglais.
items.random_wrong: Les maths sont moins difficiles que l'anglais.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Les maths sont plus difficiles que l'anglais.. “Les maths est plus difficile que l'anglais.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: les devoirs. “la récréation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many lessons typically make up a French collège school day (matin + après-midi, approximate standard)?
items.why: The real figure is 6 lessons. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
